executive_summary: Weekly sales ex-VAT of 349,000.23 with an overall margin of 17.0% for the week ending 20 June 2026.
Total waste of 3,259.31 represents 0.93% of sales ex-VAT, which is within acceptable range but warrants category-level scrutiny given the concentration in specific departments.

The department P&L total shows a highly distorted picture: combined departmental sales of 120,698.94 with a reported COGS of -15,959.48, producing a margin of 113.22% — this is driven by stock movements where closing stock significantly exceeds opening stock plus purchases, most notably in Cigarettes and fresh meat, and must be investigated for data integrity.

Cigarettes closing stock of 127,896.58 against opening stock of 114,751.02 with no purchases recorded is producing a negative COS of -13,145.56 and a reported margin of 267.91%, which is clearly a data or stock-count anomaly requiring immediate review.

Beef similarly shows a closing stock of 9,554.63 versus opening of 1,369.36 with no purchases recorded, generating a negative COS of -8,185.27 and a reported margin of 157.5% — purchases data appears to be missing across all departments, which is the root cause of the distorted P&L.
Provisions waste of 701.94 is the single largest waste line at the category level and merits urgent operational attention given the relatively modest sales base of 25,907.49.
Fish waste of 428.52 against sales of 5,406.12 represents a significant waste exposure estimated at approximately 7.9% of Fish sales, which is well above any acceptable threshold.

With purchases data absent across all ten departments, this week's departmental gross profit and margin figures cannot be relied upon for decision-making and a full data reconciliation with the buying system must be completed before the P&L is finalised.
biggest_wins: Provisions delivered sales of 25,907.49 with a scan margin of 19.91% and a near-zero recorded COS of 36.72, suggesting strong sell-through and minimal stockholding risk this week.
Produce achieved sales of 40,998.04, the highest of any fresh department, with a scan margin of 18.9%, making it the largest revenue contributor in the fresh estate.

Deli recorded a scan margin of 39.82% against sales of 9,617.54, the strongest scan margin across all departments, indicating solid product mix and pricing integrity if supported by the underlying purchases data.

Poultry delivered sales of 12,781.98 with a positive reported profit of 14,040.34 despite a scan margin of only 16.84%, and waste of just 32.73, reflecting tight stock management in a high-volume fresh category.
Overall waste percentage of 0.93% against total sales ex-VAT is a controlled result at store level, demonstrating effective daily waste management disciplines across the majority of departments.
biggest_risks: Purchases data is missing for all ten departments in this week's P&L — without purchase figures, COGS, gross profit, and margin calculations are unreliable across the entire department P&L and must be reconciled with the purchasing system as a priority before any commercial decisions are made.

Cigarettes is reporting a closing stock of 127,896.58 against an opening stock of 114,751.02 with zero purchases, producing a negative COS of -13,145.56 and a margin of 267.91% — this is not a genuine trading result and points to either a stock-count error, a misposted delivery, or a system feed issue that must be corrected this week.

Beef shows a closing stock of 9,554.63 against an opening stock of 1,369.36 with no purchases recorded, creating a negative COS of -8,185.27 — either a significant unrecorded delivery has landed or the stock count is materially overstated; the butchery manager must recount and reconcile immediately.

Fish waste of 428.52 against sales of 5,406.12 is approximately 7.9% of Fish sales, which is far above an acceptable waste rate for the category and indicates ordering levels, date management, or counter sell-through are not being managed effectively.

Provisions waste of 701.94 is the highest absolute waste line in the store this week; given the category's relatively self-service and ambient nature, this level of write-off requires investigation into date checking routines, ranging, and ordering frequency.

Produce waste of 1,319.28 is the largest single waste figure in the store, which is common for the category but must be benchmarked against the 18.9% scan margin to ensure overall Produce profitability is not being eroded — ordering discipline and daily culling routines must be reviewed.

Lamb is reporting a margin of 192.19% against a scan margin of 27.11% — as with Beef, the absence of purchases data and a closing stock of 1,507.63 versus an opening of 260.86 is producing a misleading result that must be corrected.

Dairy waste of 377.28 and Bread and Cakes waste of 391.33 do not have corresponding department P&L lines in this report, meaning their GP impact cannot be assessed this week — these departments must be included in next week's full P&L submission.
department_callouts: Cigarettes: Reported margin of 267.91% and negative COS of -13,145.56 are the result of a closing stock of 127,896.58 with no purchases recorded — data integrity issue, not a trading result; requires immediate stock recount and purchasing reconciliation.

Beef: Negative COS of -8,185.27 and reported margin of 157.5% are driven by a closing stock of 9,554.63 versus an opening of 1,369.36 with no purchases posted — butchery manager to recount stock and confirm all deliveries are booked in.

Lamb: Reported margin of 192.19% against a scan margin of 27.11% reflects the same missing purchases issue; closing stock of 1,507.63 is significantly higher than opening of 260.86 and must be verified.

Pork: Reported margin of 147.58% against a scan margin of 24.25% — closing stock of 1,148.07 versus opening of 382.24 with no purchases recorded; confirm all Pork deliveries are receipted in the system.

Bacon and Loose SRP: Reported margin of 128.5% against a scan margin of 10.87% — the large gap suggests purchases are unrecorded; closing stock of 802.55 versus opening of 528.78 warrants verification.

Poultry: Reported margin of 109.84% against a scan margin of 16.84% with waste of only 32.73 — Poultry is performing well on waste control; margin distortion is again attributable to missing purchases data.

Fish: Scan margin of 18.04% is reasonable for the category but waste of 428.52 against sales of 5,406.12 is a serious operational concern; order quantities and date rotation must be reviewed with the Fish counter manager immediately.

Produce: Highest sales department at 40,998.04 with a scan margin of 18.9% and waste of 1,319.28 — closing stock is not recorded this week which prevents a full COGS calculation; ensure closing stock is counted and submitted for next week.

Deli: Scan margin of 39.82% is the strongest in the store with sales of 9,617.54 and waste of 348.91; the positive COS of 516.92 is a more plausible figure than other departments, suggesting Deli purchases may be partially captured — confirm all cost lines are correctly attributed.

Provisions: Sales of 25,907.49 with a scan margin of 19.91% and waste of 701.94 — the waste figure is disproportionate to the category profile and must be broken down by sub-category to identify the specific lines driving the write-off.
action_plan: Finance and IT to investigate and resolve the missing purchases data for all ten departments as the immediate priority; no department margin should be reported or acted upon until purchase figures are correctly loaded and COGS recalculated.

Store manager to instruct the Cigarettes department to conduct a full physical stock recount by close of business Monday and reconcile against the system figure of 127,896.58; all recent deliveries must be confirmed as correctly posted.
Butchery manager to recount Beef, Lamb, and Pork closing stocks and confirm all deliveries received in the current week are receipted on the system before the P&L is resubmitted.

Fish department manager to conduct an immediate review of ordering levels versus actual sales velocity; implement a daily date check and waste-reduction protocol with the target of reducing Fish waste from the current 428.52 to below 200.00 in the coming week.

Provisions manager to produce a sub-category breakdown of the 701.94 waste figure, identify the top three lines by write-off value, and implement revised order quantities and date-checking routines for those lines by Wednesday.

Produce manager to ensure closing stock is counted and submitted every Saturday without fail; this week's missing closing stock has prevented a full COGS calculation for the store's highest-turnover fresh department.

Department managers for Dairy and Bread and Cakes to be included in the weekly P&L reporting cycle from next week; waste of 377.28 and 391.33 respectively cannot be assessed without corresponding sales and margin data.

Deli manager to verify that all purchase invoices for the week are correctly attributed to the Deli department and that the 39.82% scan margin is being achieved at the till through accurate PLU usage and portion control.

Waste management: schedule a store-wide waste review meeting for Tuesday involving all fresh department managers, focusing on Produce (1,319.28), Provisions (701.94), Fish (428.52), Bread and Cakes (391.33), and Dairy (377.28) as the five highest waste lines.

Ordering review: with summer trading underway, all fresh department managers to review their forward order patterns for the week commencing 22 June to ensure stock levels are matched to anticipated footfall without generating additional waste.
Ensure the weekly P&L resubmission with corrected purchases data is completed no later than Wednesday 24 June so that an accurate overall margin can be confirmed and presented to area management.

Store manager to establish a weekly pre-close stock-count checklist to ensure all departments submit both opening and closing stock figures on time and that all purchase invoices are booked by the Saturday system cut-off, preventing a recurrence of this week's data gaps.
